item.title: CISA Adds CVE-2026-20230 and CVE-2026-12569; Russian Signal, SharkLoader, Polymarket Incidents
Splunk CVE-2026-20253 Exploitation, Immobiliare npm Compromise, and Fake OpenAI Workspace Invites
Turla StockStay, Windchill RCE, Linux pedit COW/DirtyClone, and Miasma Supply Chain Attacks
Turla STOCKSTAY Targets Ukraine as Cellebrite Russia Use and Packagist Malware Blocking Emerge
CISA Mandates Emergency Patching for Cisco, Splunk, PTC Flaws Amid Miasma npm Supply Chain Attack and Signal Phishing Campaigns
item.content:encoded:
Critical Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution in Splunk Enterprise (CVE-2026-20253) — Must Read

A critical unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability (CVE-2026-20253) in Splunk Enterprise allows attackers to exploit a PostgreSQL sidecar service recovery endpoint via Splunk Web to achieve arbitrary file write and code execution; active exploitation has been confirmed, and federal agencies must patch immediately.

  • CVE-2026-20253 is a critical unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in Splunk Enterprise caused by missing authentication on a PostgreSQL sidecar service recovery endpoint reachable via the Splunk Web proxy.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-20253 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on June 18, 2026, ordering federal agencies to remediate by June 21, 2026 under Binding Operational Directive 26-04.
  • Attackers can chain path traversal, connection string injection, and reuse of the .pgpass credential file to write arbitrary files and execute code as the Splunk service account.
  • Splunk Enterprise versions 10.2.x prior to 10.2.4 and 10.0.x prior to 10.0.7 are affected, while version 9.4 and earlier are not.
  • After compromise, an attacker can tamper with logs, harvest stored credentials, and use the Splunk host to move laterally into the victim's network.

Miasma Mini Shai-Hulud Hits ImmobiliareLabs npm Packages — Must Read

The Miasma Mini Shai-Hulud supply chain campaign has expanded to compromise legitimate `@immobiliarelabs` Backstage plugins on npm, using stealthy payloads and GitHub Actions abuse to steal developer and CI/CD secrets.

  • On June 26, 2026, 22 npm packages under the @immobiliarelabs scope, including Backstage plugins for GitLab and LDAP authentication, were published with malicious payloads as part of the Miasma Mini Shai-Hulud campaign.
  • The malicious packages execute hidden JavaScript code using a 'Phantom Gyp' trick that invokes node index.js through a binding.gyp file, avoiding reliance on preinstall or postinstall scripts.
  • The payloads steal a wide range of developer and CI/CD secrets including .env files, npm/PyPI/GitHub/Slack/AWS/Azure/GCP/Vault tokens, SSH keys, Docker credentials, and Kubernetes configs, and exfiltrate them via the GitHub API.
  • A compromised third-party GitHub Action, codfish/semantic-release-action, is a high-priority lead as a possible upstream access path, after it was force-pushed with malicious commits that executed obfuscated JavaScript in downstream CI/CD runners.
  • Attackers used GitHub Actions deployment triggers to execute workflows without modifying the workflow file itself, potentially bypassing the 'workflow' OAuth scope and compromising npm publishing credentials and repository secrets.

CISA sets urgent deadline to fix Cisco flaw exploited in attacks — Must Read

CISA has issued an urgent directive for federal agencies to patch a critical, actively exploited Cisco Unified Communications Manager vulnerability (CVE-2026-20230) and a remote code execution flaw in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM (CVE-2026-12569) by Sunday, June 28.

  • The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has ordered federal agencies to patch CVE-2026-20230 and CVE-2026-12569 by Sunday, June 28.
  • CVE-2026-20230 is a critical server-side request forgery in Cisco Unified Communications Manager that is now being actively exploited to write arbitrary text files to affected systems.
  • CVE-2026-12569 is a critical remote code execution vulnerability in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM caused by improper input validation during deserialization.
  • Both vulnerabilities were added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog under Binding Operational Directive 26-04, mandating emergency remediation.

FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys — Must Read

The FBI and CISA warn that Russian intelligence hackers are phishing Signal users for their Backup Recovery Key, enabling persistent access to message history and account takeover; anyone who shared it must generate a new one immediately.

  • The FBI and CISA updated a prior warning to note that Russian intelligence hackers are now targeting Signal users' Backup Recovery Keys in phishing campaigns.
  • A stolen Backup Recovery Key allows attackers to restore backups, read private and group message history, and take over the account; the key remains effective even if a new account is created on the same phone number.
  • The phishing messages impersonate Signal support and instruct targets to paste their Recovery Key into the chat, using pretexts like mandatory two-factor authentication rollouts or urgent data recovery.
  • The campaign targets individuals of high intelligence value, including current and former U.S. and international government officials, military personnel, journalists, and Ukrainian officials, and has already compromised thousands of accounts worldwide.
  • The primary defense is to generate a new Recovery Key in Settings to invalidate the old key, but any data previously exfiltrated cannot be recovered.

New SharkLoader Malware Deploys Cobalt Strike in StrikeShark Cyberattacks — Must Read

A newly discovered campaign named StrikeShark is using the undocumented SharkLoader malware to deliver Cobalt Strike Beacon, targeting government, diplomatic, and software development organizations across Asia, the Middle East, and South America by exploiting known vulnerabilities in public-facing applications.

  • A newly discovered campaign called StrikeShark delivers the previously undocumented SharkLoader malware to load Cobalt Strike Beacon on compromised systems.
  • The campaign targeted a diplomatic organization in Indonesia, government organizations in Taiwan, and software development companies in multiple countries, along with entities in Hong Kong, Lebanon, Syria, Colombia, North Macedonia, Nepal, and Serbia.
  • Attackers gained initial access by exploiting known vulnerabilities in public-facing applications, including ProxyLogon in Microsoft Exchange, an Openfire path traversal flaw, and a GeoServer remote code execution bug.
  • The threat actor is assessed to be Chinese-speaking, based on its use of open-source tools like FScan and Pillager commonly associated with Chinese-speaking developers, though no direct link to a known group was found.
  • No data exfiltration was observed, but the targeting of government and software organizations suggests likely cyber espionage aimed at political intelligence or intellectual property.

CISA Adds Exploited PTC Windchill RCE Flaw to KEV as Web Shell Attacks Continue — Must Read

CISA added a critical remote code execution vulnerability (CVE-2026-12569) in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation involving JSP web shells, and has released indicators of compromise and mitigations.

  • The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) added CVE-2026-12569, a critical remote code execution flaw in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM software, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog.
  • The vulnerability stems from improper input validation and can be exploited through deserialization of untrusted data to execute arbitrary code.
  • Despite patches being released last week, PTC confirmed continued active exploitation as of June 25, with attackers deploying JSP web shells on affected systems.
  • PTC released indicators of compromise including attacker IP addresses, a web shell file hash, and a naming pattern, along with mitigation steps like blocking a command-and-control address and scanning for suspicious JSP files.
  • This is the first PTC product vulnerability added to CISA's KEV catalog, highlighting the rapid weaponization of newly disclosed vulnerabilities.

Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions in Supply Chain Attack — Must Read

The Miasma malware family has expanded its supply chain attack to npm and Go packages, steals credentials via GitHub Actions compromise, and propagates across developer environments.

  • Attackers used a compromised maintainer account to push malicious versions of LeoPlatform and RStreams npm packages within a six-second window.
  • The malware executes via a binding.gyp file during package installation, downloads and installs the Bun runtime, and then runs a stealer payload to harvest credentials.
  • A malicious GitHub Actions workflow named "Run Copilot" is dropped to steal CI/CD secrets, which are uploaded to a public repository with the description "Alright Lets See If This Works."
  • The campaign also compromised the codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action on June 24, 2026 by force-pushing a malicious commit and redirecting version tags.
  • The malware includes a Russian locale killswitch, checks for the presence of endpoint security software, and polls GitHub hourly for commits containing "firedalazer" to retrieve the Hades variant.

Google Details Turla's New STOCKSTAY Backdoor Used in Ukraine Espionage Attacks — Must Read

Google reveals Turla's new STOCKSTAY backdoor, a .NET-based malware sharing code with Kazuar, used in espionage attacks against Ukrainian government and military organizations and entities interested in Italian foreign policy.

  • The Russian state-sponsored group Turla has deployed a previously undocumented .NET backdoor named STOCKSTAY against Ukrainian government and military organizations and entities interested in Italian foreign policy.
  • STOCKSTAY shares significant code and functional overlaps with Turla's existing Kazuar backdoor, with development activity dating back to December 2022.
  • The malware consists of distinct components that communicate via WM_COPYDATA messages and connect to command-and-control servers through secure WebSocket using the open-source websocket-sharp library.
  • Attack campaigns delivered STOCKSTAY through phishing emails with malicious RDP files and exploitation of the WinRAR vulnerability CVE-2025-8088, as recently as November 2025.
  • STOCKSTAY has been used for initial access into unprofiled environments and during post-exploitation after reconnaissance, sometimes deployed alongside Kazuar.

Multiple @immobiliarelabs Backstage Plugins Compromised on npm — Must Read

Multiple @immobiliarelabs Backstage plugins on npm were compromised with a malicious payload that executes via a node-gyp binding.gyp hook during installation, stealing credentials and persisting in AI coding tools. The attack is part of the Miasma campaign and affects all major version lines.

  • On June 26, 2026, compromised versions of four @immobiliarelabs Backstage plugins were published to npm, containing a malicious payload that fires at install time.
  • The payload uses a binding.gyp file to trigger node-gyp execution of a 5 MB index.js, bypassing tools that only monitor package.json postinstall scripts.
  • The payload steals credentials from GitHub Actions secrets, cloud provider credentials, package registry tokens, password manager databases, SSH keys, and more.
  • The payload attempts to persist by modifying configuration files of AI coding assistants, including Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, VS Code, Aider, and others.
  • This attack follows the pattern of the Miasma campaign, employing the same binding.gyp technique to execute malicious code during npm install.

Chinese-Speaking APT Deploys New TinyRCT Backdoor in Southeast Asia Campaign — Must Read

A Chinese-speaking APT group (CL-STA-1062/UAT-7237) has deployed a previously undocumented custom backdoor, TinyRCT, in a campaign targeting government entities and critical infrastructure across Southeast Asia, using open-source tools like SoftEther VPN and Mimikatz for initial access and lateral movement.

  • A Chinese-speaking APT group tracked as CL-STA-1062 (overlapping with UAT-7237) has deployed a new custom backdoor called TinyRCT in a campaign targeting government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia.
  • TinyRCT is a .NET remote access trojan that can execute commands, enumerate and exfiltrate files, capture the victim's screen, and delete itself, communicating with its C2 server over AES-encrypted HTTP beaconing.
  • The attackers used web shells for initial foothold and combined open-source tools such as SoftEther VPN, Mimikatz, VNT, and Yuze with the custom backdoor to move laterally and maintain access.
  • The campaign compromised at least 10 organizations in Southeast Asia between October and December 2025, including the exfiltration of an entire directory of web server source code from a government entity and data from an MS SQL server.
  • CL-STA-1062 has been active in East Asia since at least March 2022, with recent activity beginning mid-2025 focusing on critical infrastructure and government targets in the region.

New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries — Must Read

A local privilege escalation vulnerability (CVE-2026-46331) in the Linux kernel's traffic-control subsystem allows unprivileged users to gain root by corrupting cached binaries in memory, with a public exploit available. Immediate mitigation requires patching, disabling the act_pedit kernel module, or turning off unprivileged user namespaces.

  • CVE-2026-46331 is an out-of-bounds write in the Linux kernel's act_pedit traffic-control action that corrupts shared page-cache memory, enabling a local unprivileged user to escalate to root.
  • A public exploit surfaced within a day of the CVE assignment, poisoning the in-memory cached copy of a setuid root binary like /bin/su and executing a payload with root privileges without modifying the file on disk.
  • The exploit requires the act_pedit kernel module to be loadable and unprivileged user namespaces to be enabled, conditions that were present on tested RHEL and Debian systems.
  • Affected distributions include RHEL 8/9/10, Debian 11/12/13, and Ubuntu 18.04 through 26.04, though Ubuntu 26.04 blocks the exploit path by default through AppArmor policies restricting unprivileged user namespaces.
  • File-integrity checks may miss the attack because corruption occurs only in memory, and dropping the page cache removes the altered copy but does not terminate an already opened root shell.

Russia used social engineering to breach prominent messaging accounts, Ukraine says — Must Read

Ukraine's SBU and the FBI uncovered a Russian social engineering campaign that impersonates messaging platform support to steal account credentials from government, military, and activist targets, exploiting users' morning vulnerability.

  • Ukraine's SBU, in collaboration with the FBI, uncovered a long-running Russian campaign to compromise the messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians, and activists.
  • The attackers used social engineering techniques, such as text messages impersonating platform support services sent in the morning to exploit users' vulnerability, rather than exploiting app flaws.
  • The campaign aimed to steal sensitive military, political, and economic information from messaging platforms and to collect victims' personal data, targeting individuals in Ukraine, Europe, and the United States.
  • The SBU did not identify the specific Russian intelligence service responsible, the main messaging platforms targeted, or the total number of victims.
  • Earlier this year, Dutch intelligence agencies warned of Russian state-backed hackers conducting a similar global campaign to hijack Signal and WhatsApp accounts by tricking victims into sharing verification codes.

Turla group adds more malware to Russia’s espionage efforts against Ukraine — Must Read

Russia's Turla group (linked to the FSB) has been using the StockStay malware since late 2022 to conduct cyber-espionage against Ukrainian government and defense organizations, as well as some European targets, maintaining redundant malware ecosystems for persistent access.

  • Russian state-backed hacking group Turla, also tracked as Secret Blizzard and Venomous Bear and linked to the FSB, is behind the StockStay malware which has been under active development since at least December 2022.
  • StockStay was primarily used to spy on Ukrainian government and defense organizations, with early samples also identified in Italy, the Netherlands, Poland, and Germany.
  • The malware shares significant code and functionality with the older Kazuar malware, reflecting a strategy of building redundant, parallel malware ecosystems to maintain persistent access.
  • Victims were typically infected through phishing emails containing malicious RDP configuration files that connected their computers to attacker-controlled infrastructure, and the malware has evolved from disguising as a stock market app to masquerading as PDF readers and calculators.
  • Turla used academic and diplomatic lures, including a compromised Ukrainian university email account and a diplomatic education platform, to distribute malicious emails and files.

New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets — Should Read

A new Linux kernel privilege escalation flaw, CVE-2026-43503 (DirtyClone), lets local users gain root by exploiting missing safety flags during network packet cloning, and has been patched in upstream and distribution kernels.

  • DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503) is a Linux kernel privilege escalation that lets a local user gain root by corrupting file-backed memory through a cloned network packet.
  • The vulnerability is caused by kernel helper functions dropping a safety flag that marks packet memory as shared with a file, turning a performance optimization into a write primitive.
  • The attack uses an attacker-controlled IPsec tunnel to overwrite the in-memory copy of a privileged binary without changing the file on disk, evading file-integrity tools and leaving no audit trail.
  • On Debian and Fedora, unprivileged user namespaces are enabled by default, allowing a local user to obtain the required CAP_NET_ADMIN capability; Ubuntu 24.04 and later block this default path via AppArmor.
  • This is the fourth DirtyFrag-family flaw, all sharing the same failure mode of dropped shared-frag flags during fragment transfers; a multi-site fix was merged on May 21 and assigned CVE-2026-43503.

Amazon Q Developer Flaw Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs — Should Read

A high-severity vulnerability (CVE-2026-12957) in Amazon Q Developer's MCP configuration handling allowed malicious repositories to automatically execute commands and steal cloud credentials; users must update their IDE plugins to the patched versions immediately.

  • A high-severity flaw (CVE-2026-12957) in Amazon Q Developer allowed a malicious repository to run arbitrary commands and steal cloud credentials by placing a specially crafted MCP configuration file in the workspace.
  • The vulnerability resided in Language Servers for AWS, the runtime that powers Amazon Q across VS Code, JetBrains, Eclipse, and Visual Studio, and was fixed in version 1.65.0, with AWS recommending an update to 1.69.0 that also addresses a second flaw (CVE-2026-12958).
  • Amazon's patch now requires explicit user consent before launching MCP servers from untrusted workspaces, preventing automatic command execution.
  • There is no known public exploitation of this flaw, according to CISA's ADP entry.
  • The article highlights a recurring pattern of similar MCP configuration trust issues in other AI coding assistants such as Claude Code, Cursor, and Windsurf.

FCC votes to toughen rules in bid to better protect undersea cables — Should Read

The FCC voted to tighten oversight of undersea internet cables, barring Chinese and other foreign-adversary equipment and requiring licensing for critical terminal gear, while fast-tracking approvals for vetted U.S. operators to counter espionage and physical threats.

  • The FCC voted to mandate licensing for owners and operators of submarine line terminal equipment (SLTE) to close oversight gaps in a vulnerable part of undersea cable networks.
  • The new rules will ban equipment from anywhere in China and other foreign adversary nations, expanding a prior block on specific companies like Huawei, China Telecom, ZTE, and China Mobile.
  • Operators who certify high security standards, have operated cables without incident, and avoid insecure foreign equipment can receive exemptions from intensive review to speed up approvals.
  • Undersea cables carry up to 99% of global internet traffic and face growing threats from espionage (such as the Salt Typhoon hacks) and physical damage (including Russian submarine activity near the UK and attacks in the Baltic Sea).

Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff — Should Read

Russian authorities continued using Cellebrite's offline-capable forensic tools to extract data from an activist's phone months after the company halted sales to Russia, proving that a sales ban alone fails to neutralize previously deployed hardware.

  • Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to extract data from the iPhone of detained activist Andrey Pivovarov in June 2021.
  • The extraction occurred three months after Cellebrite announced it would stop selling its tools to Russia and Belarus.
  • Evidence includes a trusted USB pairing record matching a Cellebrite fingerprint and a Russian government forensic report that names Cellebrite's UFED Physical Analyzer and UFED 4PC.
  • Investigators searched the extracted data for political contacts, opposition figures, and organizations such as Open Russia.
  • Cellebrite's sales cutoff left existing offline-capable hardware operational, allowing Russia to continue using the tool on seized devices.

Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant — Should Read

A phishing campaign targets hotels with fake booking complaints, using a photo-themed ZIP file to deliver a Node.js backdoor (TonRAT) that evades email security via authentication laundering through Calendly and Google redirects, securing persistent access to front-desk systems.

  • A phishing campaign using photo-themed ZIP files has been targeting hotel and hospitality organizations across Europe and Asia since April 2026.
  • Attackers exploited Calendly's email notification system and Google's URL redirect service to pass email authentication checks, a technique Microsoft calls authentication laundering.
  • The infection chain delivers a Node.js implant called TonRAT that fetches command-and-control domains from the TON blockchain and maintains persistent access through registry keys.
  • The attackers' ultimate objective remains unclear, with no confirmed data theft, ransomware, or named victims reported by Microsoft.
  • Remediation requires removing both the RunOnce and Node.js Run registry keys along with the Node.js runtime and JavaScript files, as leaving one component active can restore the threat.

Polymarket customers lose $3 million in supply-chain attack — Should Read

A supply-chain attack on Polymarket's frontend injected malicious JavaScript through a compromised third-party vendor, tricking users into approving fraudulent transactions and resulting in approximately $3 million in stolen funds from fewer than 15 accounts.

  • Polymarket says it will fully reimburse customers who lost an estimated $3 million after a supply-chain attack injected malicious JavaScript into its website via a compromised third-party vendor.
  • The injected script tricked users into approving fraudulent transactions on the official Polymarket site, while the platform's own servers and backend were not breached.
  • Blockchain security firm PeckShield described the incident as a phishing campaign that stole roughly $3 million in ParyonUSD, later swapped for about 1,893 Ether.
  • According to Bubblemaps, the theft impacted fewer than 15 accounts, and the firm published affected account addresses and wallet addresses holding the stolen funds.
  • Polymarket did not disclose many details and had not responded to a request for comment by publication time.

Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites — Should Read

Attackers are impersonating cybersecurity companies by creating fraudulent OpenAI tenants and sending legitimate platform invitations to trick employees into joining, likely to harvest sensitive data submitted in AI chats and projects.

  • Threat actors are creating fraudulent OpenAI tenants that impersonate legitimate companies and invite employees to join, a campaign Push Security dubs the "Poisoned Tenant" attack.
  • Invitation emails originate from OpenAI's legitimate notification infrastructure, pass email authentication checks, and are visually identical to genuine invitations, making them likely to bypass security filters.
  • Once an employee accepts, they are given Owner privileges in the attacker-controlled tenant, which contains an account impersonating the company's CEO and has a payment card attached to appear legitimate.
  • The likely goal is to trick employees into using the fake ChatGPT workspace as a trusted corporate platform, enabling attackers to collect sensitive information such as source code, internal documents, or strategic plans.
  • The campaign has targeted cybersecurity and technology firms, with attackers researching individual employees' work email addresses to personalize the invitations.

Packagist is now protected by Aikido Intel and other updates to the PHP registry — Should Read

Packagist now blocks known malicious PHP package versions by default in Composer 2.10+ using the open Aikido Intel feed, stopping accidental malware installs across the entire PHP ecosystem.

  • Composer 2.10 and higher now blocks known malicious package versions by default, using malware flags from the Aikido Intel feed.
  • The Aikido Intel feed is CC-BY licensed, automated, and flags malicious versions in minutes, allowing near-instant blocking without manual intervention.
  • Packagist enforces stable version immutability, meaning a published stable version can no longer be rewritten, which ends re-tagging attacks entirely.
  • Private Packagist can require a minimum Composer version to connect and refuses to serve flagged package files to any client, protecting even old or un-updated toolchains.
  • Other package registries, including npm, PyPI, crates.io, and RubyGems, are also adopting structural security measures like staged publishing, mandatory 2FA, and trusted publishing.

A Construction Bid Invitation Hid a Compromised Website Behind a Legitimate-Looking PDF Label — May Read

A phishing email disguised as a construction bid invitation used a link that appeared to be a PDF but actually pointed to a compromised Brazilian website, bypassing email authentication. The attack was detected solely by the mismatch between the anchor text and the actual URL destination.

  • The phishing email was formatted as a construction bid invitation, sent via BCC to hide the recipient list and make each target think the message was addressed only to them.
  • The link in the email displayed text appearing as a bid-preview PDF filename, but the actual URL behind it pointed to a compromised Brazilian media website with no connection to construction.
  • All email authentication mechanisms (SPF, DKIM, ARC) passed, and the sending domain had no reputation issues, meaning gateway checks alone would not have blocked the message.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI detected the attack by identifying the mismatch between the link's visible text and its true destination, quarantining the email before delivery.
  • The attack relied on a compromised third-party website to host the phishing path, bypassing filters that focus on domain age or known-bad reputation.

Russia accuses Apple of ‘political censorship’ after VK apps removed from App Store — May Read

Russia accuses Apple of political censorship after the App Store removal of VK's social media and messaging apps; Apple states the action complies with sanctions regulations, without specifying which ones.

  • Apple removed multiple VK apps—including VKontakte, VK Music, VK Messenger, VK Video, Odnoklassniki, and Mail.ru services—from the App Store, preventing users from downloading or updating them.
  • Apple told BBC News Russia that it removed the applications to comply with sanctions regulations and that it follows the laws of the jurisdictions in which it operates, but it did not specify which sanctions applied.
  • Russian authorities, including Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov and Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova, accused Apple of political censorship.
  • VK said the removal was done without prior notice or explanation, that it had never been placed under sanctions, and that it had provided legal opinions to Apple supporting that position.
  • Apple previously removed VK’s apps from the App Store in September 2022 after Britain sanctioned senior VK executives, then restored them less than a month later.

Time Travel Debugging with Codex — Optional

Giving Codex access to Time Travel Debugging (TTD) traces via TTDObjectsPy allowed it to extract ground-truth runtime state and solve a heavily obfuscated reverse engineering challenge correctly and efficiently, whereas static analysis alone led to incorrect answers.

  • Codex without Time Travel Debugging attempted to solve the FlareAuthenticator challenge by patching and forcing a success path, resulting in an incorrect flag after 1 hour 51 minutes and 69.5 million tokens.
  • Codex with TTDObjectsPy captured execution traces that revealed the actual validation state comparison and per-digit delta contributions, enabling constraint solving that produced the correct flag in about 50 minutes and 16.4 million tokens.
  • TTDObjectsPy wraps Microsoft's Time Travel Debugging APIs to give an LLM queryable access to register/memory state, function calls, and memory writes from recorded traces.
  • The author cautions that TTD adds operational costs such as disk space, capture reliability, and cleanup, and is most useful when critical behavior depends on runtime state rather than static structure.

Guardian Agents: The Next Layer of Identity Governance — Optional

Traditional identity governance tools fail to secure AI agents because they enforce controls only at authentication time, leaving autonomous agents unmonitored as they operate freely across systems—enterprises need runtime governance that tracks behavior in real time.

  • AI agents inherit the full permissions of the human or service identity they operate on behalf of, dynamically selecting tools and chaining calls across multiple systems, which breaks the static access model of traditional identity governance.
  • Traditional IAM tools only govern access at authentication time and remain blind to the runtime behavior of AI agents, including their tool calls, data accesses, and cross-system traversals after login.
  • A guardian agent is an autonomous control layer that continuously discovers AI agents, maps their identities and permission inheritance, baselines their behavior, and enforces least-privilege policies at runtime.
  • Unmanaged AI agents become 'identity dark matter,' accumulating over-privileged access, orphaned credentials, and exposure to prompt injection attacks that enable privilege escalation without credential compromise.
  • Effective governance requires deploying discovery mechanisms to inventory all AI agents, classifying them by risk, enforcing runtime least-privilege, and integrating agent identity telemetry into existing IAM and IGA platforms.

Open NDR and Threat Intelligence for Modern Detection | Corelight — Optional

Corelight promotes its Open NDR platform, which integrates threat intelligence to enhance network detection and response for modern security operations.

  • Corelight offers an Open NDR platform for network detection and response.
  • The Open NDR platform integrates threat intelligence to improve threat detection.
  • The article highlights Corelight's commercial solution for modern security operations.

Top Koi alternatives in 2026 — Optional

With Koi now acquired by Palo Alto Networks, teams seeking developer device supply chain protection may face integration complexity and roadmap uncertainty; Aikido Security is positioned as the best alternative, offering broader endpoint coverage and a full SDLC platform with transparent pricing.

  • Palo Alto Networks acquired Koi Security in April 2026 and plans to integrate its technology into Cortex XDR and Prisma AIRS, creating uncertainty around future pricing, packaging, and roadmap.
  • Koi's protection is limited to governing what software developers install on endpoints, with no visibility into application security weaknesses like source code vulnerabilities, hardcoded secrets, or cloud misconfigurations.
  • Aikido Security is presented as the leading alternative, offering a default 48-hour minimum package age policy to block newly published malicious packages, an on-device agent that works offline, and a full platform spanning SAST, SCA, secrets, IaC, containers, DAST, AI pentesting, and cloud posture.
  • Koi does not publish pricing or offer a self-serve trial, requiring a sales-led evaluation, whereas Aikido provides transparent pricing and a free tier.

Your First GRC Agent: A Red Teamer's Walkthrough — Optional

Agentic AI transforms GRC from periodic snapshot compliance to continuous, autonomous monitoring with auditable logs, allowing teams to respond to control drift in real time rather than waiting for scheduled reviews.

  • An agent differs from schedule-based automation by acting autonomously when a condition is met, using the actual state of the program, and executing multiple analysis-decision-action steps in sequence.
  • A practical GRC agent can be built by picking a trigger (e.g., evidence older than 24 hours), describing the work in plain English, and deploying it with a full audit trail that logs every step.
  • Observability is ensured through an execution log that records the trigger, inputs, evaluation, decision, action, and timestamps, making any decision reconstructable for auditors without trusting the agent blindly.
  • Safe deployment requires giving the agent read-only access to evaluated systems, write access only to designated GRC objects, and routing consequential actions like closing a risk to a human for sign-off.
  • The author advises starting with high-toil, low-judgment tasks such as evidence gap detection to build trust before expanding to higher-stakes controls.

Healthcare Cybersecurity in an Era of Geopolitical Instability — Optional

European healthcare organizations must now treat cybersecurity as a resilience function shaped by geopolitical instability, not merely as technical protection, because threat exposure and supply-chain dependencies are directly affected by contested statecraft and military conflicts.

  • Healthcare has traditionally been viewed as a protected, civilian, neutral, and essential sector, but that assumption is increasingly being tested.
  • The current geopolitical environment is marked by sustained instability, with multiple military conflicts and rising tensions across regions.
  • Cyber operations have become a persistent instrument of statecraft.
  • These geopolitical and cyber developments are interconnected, and healthcare is no longer outside their scope.

Rolldown Pulls Rust React Compiler Integration After Binary Size Increase — Optional

Rolldown and Vite maintainers pulled a Rust-based React Compiler integration after determining the 5MB binary size increase was unjustifiable for all users, opting instead to explore a smaller opt-in implementation.

  • Rolldown and Vite maintainers pulled a Rust-based React Compiler integration after determining that the 17% binary size increase (from 28.7MB to 33.8MB) could not be justified for a feature that would ship to all users by default.
  • The Oxc PR integrating the Rust React Compiler increased the Oxc napi transform addon from 3.51 MiB to 8.66 MiB, and Boshen later reduced that by 759 KiB by eliminating a JSON serialization round trip.
  • Evan You argued that Vite is framework-agnostic and cannot add React-specific bloat because it would set a precedent for other framework-specific additions and impose a 5MB download cost on millions of non-React users.
  • A new napi-rs proposal suggests an optional compress flag that could shrink a native addon's footprint from 25.74MB to 8.15MB on darwin-arm64 with no runtime speed impact.
  • Boshen is now experimenting with a smaller implementation that shows up to 2x performance improvement with only a 1.4MB binary size increase.

item.title: Poland arrests SIM-swapping gang; Anthropic tests mobile Claude Cowork
Shop Callback Phishing, SaaS Cookie Theft, and Windows 10 ESU Updates
